Barnes searches the Georgics for clues to what happened at Taren after conquest, the attraction of its wealth for settlers, early and lat for colonists and for Rome's elite, including Augustan poets. Ver (Georgics 2. 149-150) signals that Italy's "spring is perpetual, summer extends to months other than her own; twice a year the cow calve, twice a year the trees serve us fruit." Scholars today dubious about the reliability of Vergil's claims for Ital productivity. Fox recalls that Jerome considered Vergil a worth authority on the pregnancy of sheep and goats twice annually and len the master poet's weight to his explication of a passage in Gene where Jacob and Laban were involved in a dispute about the divis of herds.

" Mantles woven with gold: Pallas ' shroud and Vergilian Bibliography 2003-2004 143 the End of the Aeneid." C J 99 (2003-4) 135-156. Gross focuses on Pallas' funeral rites and Pallas as immolation, and offers an explanation of Aeneas' controversial statement at the close. "By killing Turnus, Aeneas in a very real sense annihilates himself (and his past). Turnus is Aeneas' alter ego." Bleisch accents the crucial significance of the Regia passage for the epic. Marinčič relates the Arcadia theme of Eclogue 4 to the historical myth of Hercules and Cacus. Galinsky interprets the end of the Aeneid as Vergil's reworking of Roman historical epic in response to themes of Greek tragic drama.

raw_text: Otto Zwierlein. Die Ovid- und Vergil- Revision in tiberischer Zeit. Band I. Prolegomena. Berlin/New York 1999. Rev: Galinsky, Gnomon 74 (2002) 685-687. Jamarťs article offers a probing parallel study of Vergil's Camilla and Statius' Parthenopaeus. McGill provides instances of author who sought to rework and reinterpret Aeneid themes. Som approached the epic as poetry capable of being made anew, recasting language and content with varying degrees of fidelity to the original. Pagán argues that both anachronistic ecphrases enabled the poet and historian to deal with "moments of extreme cultural and political crisis"(58). Pattimo's study of satiric degradation highlight Petronius' pilot and Vergil's Palinurus. The Watson volume of Martial's epigrams attaches particular interest to Epigram 8.5 (number 15, in their collection). Panoussi targets sinister contrast between Lucan 3 and Aeneid 6 (a case of deforestation to fuel the funeral pyre of Misenus) and the sea battle at Actium in Aeneid 8.

raw_text: Robin Sowerby. "The Augustan Aeneis: Virgil Enlightened?" Translation and Literature 1 1 (2002) 237-269. [Dryden] Ortiz's article treats the terrifying figure of Oroonoko, a fictional West African prince sold into slavery who featured in Aphra Behn's novella in 1688. Behn's story was reworked in 1695 in Thomas Southerne's tragedy, and most recently in Biyi Bandele's 1999 version. Behn's novella has been remodelled into drama, poetry, and even abolitionist propaganda. Ortiz realizes that Oroonoko: Or the Royal Slave, is a masterpiece of prose fiction with Vergilian traits.
